nodes.title: CIS 2910 Review: Sets, Functions, and Proofs
nodes.content: A comprehensive review covering the fundamental mathematical structures of sets and functions, alongside formal proof techniques and best practices.

nodes.title: Set Theory Basics
nodes.content: Foundational concepts regarding collections of objects, their notations, and basic properties.

nodes.title: Set and Element
nodes.content: A set is a collection of objects; each object is an element. Denoted by roster notation {a, b} or set-builder notation.

nodes.title: Cardinality
nodes.content: The number of distinct elements in a finite set A, denoted by |A|.

nodes.title: Finite vs. Infinite Sets
nodes.content: A finite set is empty or has n elements; an infinite set is not finite.

nodes.title: Common Numerical Sets
nodes.content: Includes Natural numbers (N), Integers (Z), Rational numbers (Q), and Real numbers (R).

nodes.title: Subset & Proper Subset
nodes.content: A is a subset of B (A ⊆ B) if every element of A is in B. It is a proper subset (A ⊂ B) if A ⊆ B and A ≠ B.

nodes.title: Power Set
nodes.content: The set of all subsets of a set A, denoted P(A). For a set with n elements, |P(A)| = 2^n.

nodes.title: Set Operations
nodes.content: Union (A ∪ B) contains elements in A OR B. Intersection (A ∩ B) contains elements in A AND B.

nodes.title: Functions
nodes.content: Mathematical mappings between sets and the rules governing them.

nodes.title: Function Definition
nodes.content: A mapping f: X → Y where every x in domain X relates to exactly one y in target Y.

nodes.title: Domain, Target, and Range
nodes.content: Domain (X) is the input set; Target/Co-domain (Y) is the potential output set; Range is the actual set of outputs.

nodes.title: Well-defined Function
nodes.content: A function is well-defined if it maps each domain element to exactly one target element.

nodes.title: Function Equality
nodes.content: Two functions are equal if they share the same domain, target, and f(x) = g(x) for all x.

nodes.title: Mathematical Definitions
nodes.content: Key terms and properties used as the basis for proofs.

nodes.title: Parity
nodes.content: An integer is even if x = 2k, and odd if x = 2k + 1 for some integer k.

nodes.title: Rational Numbers
nodes.content: A number r is rational if it can be expressed as x/y where x, y are integers and y ≠ 0.

nodes.title: Divisibility
nodes.content: x divides y if y = kx for some integer k. x is a factor/divisor; y is a multiple.

nodes.title: Prime and Composite
nodes.content: Prime: n > 1 divisible only by 1 and n. Composite: n > 1 with a divisor m such that 1 < m < n.

nodes.title: Proof Techniques
nodes.content: Methods used to demonstrate the truth of mathematical statements.

nodes.title: Theorem and Proof
nodes.content: A theorem is a provable statement; a proof is a logical sequence of steps from axioms to conclusion.

nodes.title: Proof by Exhaustion
nodes.content: Proving a universal statement by checking every element in a small domain individually.

nodes.title: Universal Generalization
nodes.content: Proving a statement for an 'arbitrary' object in the domain to prove it for all objects.

nodes.title: Existence Proofs
nodes.content: Constructive: provides a specific example. Nonconstructive: proves existence without an example.

nodes.title: Counterexample
nodes.content: An assignment of values that proves a universal statement is false.

nodes.title: Best Practices and Errors
nodes.content: Guidelines for writing clear proofs and avoiding logical fallacies.

nodes.title: Existential Instantiation
nodes.content: Logic law: if an object exists, it can be named as long as the name is not already in use.

nodes.title: Common Proof Mistakes
nodes.content: Includes generalizing from examples, skipping steps, circular reasoning, and assuming unproven facts.

nodes.title: Proof Writing Best Practices
nodes.content: Use allowed assumptions, clear keywords, and follow a logical flow from hypothesis to conclusion.
